title: Low Back Pain — Mechanical
oneLiner: Common musculoskeletal pain localised to lumbar region; usually self-limiting (<6 weeks), but must exclude red flags for serious spinal pathology.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cauda equina syndrome (CES):** *Saddle anaesthesia + acute urinary retention (post-void residual >200 mL)* — neurosurgical emergency within 24h to preserve continence & function.
- **Spinal infection (discitis/epidural abscess):** *Fever + severe unremitting back pain + raised CRP/ESR* — can progress to sepsis, paralysis, death.
- **Malignant cord compression:** *Known cancer history + progressive leg weakness/sensory level* — need urgent MRI + steroids ± radiotherapy.
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA):** *Pulsatile abdominal mass (if palpable) + hypotension/shock + back/flank pain* — immediate surgical referral; do not wait for CT.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Axial low back pain, worse with movement (bending, lifting, twisting)  
  - No leg pain or radicular symptoms (if purely mechanical)  
  - Pain eased by lying flat or changing position  
  - Morning stiffness <30 min (if present, consider inflammatory back pain)  
  - No systemic symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss)

- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Normal neurological exam (motor, sensory, reflexes, perianal sensation, anal tone)  
  - Straight leg raise (SLR) negative bilaterally (if positive, suggests radiculopathy)  
  - Lumbar spine movement restricted by pain  
  - Paraspinal muscle spasm ± tenderness over facet joints  
  - No sacroiliac joint tenderness (may indicate SIJ dysfunction)  
  - No focal bone tenderness (if present, consider fracture)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely**  
  1. Mechanical low back pain (non-specific) — no leg symptoms, no red flags, normal exam  
  2. Radiculopathy (sciatica) — leg pain/paraesthesia, positive SLR, may have reflex/sensory loss  
  3. Lumbar spinal stenosis — neurogenic claudication (relieved by sitting/leaning forward)  
  4. Inflammatory back pain (ankylosing spondylitis) — young adult, morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise, sacroiliitis on imaging  
  5. Sacroiliac joint dysfunction — unilateral buttock/leg pain, pain on SIJ provocation tests  
  6. **Red flag differentials** (cauda equina, infection, malignancy, fracture, AAA) — see Killers First

- **Splitter:**  
  Absence of leg pain + negative SLR → mechanical back pain (vs presence of leg pain + positive SLR → radiculopathy).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Full neurological exam including perianal sensation, anal tone, saddle area (every patient)  
  - Urine dipstick (UTI can mimic back pain in elderly)  
  - Pregnancy test (women of childbearing age) — refer to exclude ectopic if atypical pain  
  - Vital signs (temperature, HR, BP) — if febrile, think infection

- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, ESR — **only if red flags** (suspected infection, malignancy, inflammatory disease)  
  - U&E, bone profile — if suspicion of osteoporosis/fracture risk  
  - No routine bloods for mechanical back pain

- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** None — NICE recommends no imaging for acute non-specific low back pain without red flags.  
  - **Second-line (if red flags or >6 weeks with limited response to PT):**  
    - X-ray lumbar spine (AP + lateral) only if trauma, osteoporosis, or suspected fracture  
    - MRI spine (whole spine) for cauda equina, infection, malignancy, progressive neuro deficit  
    - CT spine if MRI contraindicated (e.g. pacemaker, claustrophobia) but less sensitive for soft tissue  
  - **Special test:** CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast if suspected AAA (but bedside US or CT angiogram if unstable)

- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Red flag checklist** (NICE): age <20 or >55, history of cancer, steroid use, IV drug use, fever, unexplained weight loss, neurological deficit, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention, band-like pain.  
  - No specific scoring system for mechanical back pain.

- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Thorough neurological examination including perianal sensory testing and anal tone** — omission of this can miss cauda equina syndrome until irreversible.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Reassure: most acute back pain resolves in 4–6 weeks with simple measures.  
  - Advise **staying active** — avoid bed rest >24h.  
  - Simple analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO QDS ± NSAID (ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS or naproxen 250–500mg PO BD) — with food, check renal function & contraindications.  
  - Heat packs for local muscle spasm.  
  - No routine imaging; no opioids unless severe pain and short course (<1 week) [check local policy].

- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Rarely admitted for mechanical back pain alone — only if severe pain uncontrolled, red flags pending investigations, or severe functional limitation.  
  - Ensure mobility with physiotherapy review.  
  - Continue simple analgesia; avoid long-term opioids.  
  - If admitted for red flag workup, manage underlying cause (e.g. IV antibiotics for spinal infection).

- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Physiotherapy (exercise, manual therapy, directional preference exercises) — refer if pain persists >4–6 weeks or recurrent.  
  - Consider group exercise programme or structured back school.  
  - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for chronic pain.  
  - For chronic mechanical back pain (>12 weeks) with limited response: consider referral to pain clinic or multidisciplinary assessment.

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: nil specific unless on opioids (ensure hydration to prevent constipation).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: only if immobile or additional risk factors (e.g. age >60, obesity, surgery); low-molecular-weight heparin per local policy.  
  - Diet: full ward diet; encourage fibre if on opioids.  
  - Observations: routine 4-hourly unless red flag suspicion; if fever, consider 1-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Paracetamol: 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day) — first choice for mild–moderate pain.  
  - Ibuprofen: 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g/day) — with or after food; avoid in asthma, GFR <30, heart failure, GI bleed history.  
  - Naproxen: 250–500mg PO BD (max 1g/day) — alternative to ibuprofen; same precautions.  
  - Codeine phosphate: 30–60mg PO Q6–8h PRN (short-term only if NSAID contraindicated or insufficient; add laxative). [Check local policy for dosing strong opioids.]

- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Tramadol 50–100mg PO Q4–6h (max 400mg/day) — avoid with SSRIs (serotonin syndrome risk).  
  - Amitriptyline 10–25mg PO nocte (for chronic pain, not acute) — start low, titrate; avoid in elderly, cardiac disease.  
  - Muscle relaxants (e.g. diazepam, baclofen) — not routinely recommended; risk of sedation, falls, dependence.

- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid in GFR <30, severe asthma, GI bleeding, uncontrolled hypertension, heart failure.  
  - Codeine/tramadol: avoid in opioid-tolerant patients, respiratory disorders, breastfeeding (codeine).  
  - Amitriptyline: caution with MAOIs, prolong QTc, history of seizures.

- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; dose reduction if 30–60; check baseline U&E.  
  - Paracetamol: safe in renal/hepatic impairment (avoid in severe liver disease).  
  - Codeine: reduce dose in renal impairment; avoid if hepatic impairment.  
  - Tramadol: reduce dose in renal/hepatic impairment [check local policy].

- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **NSAID overdose** or prescribing without checking renal function in elderly/dehydrated → acute kidney injury.  
  - **Paracetamol overdose** (unintentional or therapeutic) → hepatotoxicity; always check max daily dose (4g).  
  - **Codeine in ultra-rapid metabolisers** → opioid toxicity; avoid in children/breastfeeding.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Rare in mechanical back pain; progression to radiculopathy or neurogenic claudication.  
  - Medication-related: NSAID GI bleeding, analgesic-induced constipation.

- **Chronic:**  
  - Chronic low back pain (20–30% of acute cases) with functional limitation, disability, psychological distress.  
  - Work absence ≥4 weeks → increased risk of long-term disability.  
  - Opioid dependence if prescribed inappropriately.  
  - Deconditioning with prolonged inactivity.
sections.Prognosis:
- 50% improve within 1 week; 90% by 6 weeks.  
- 20–30% develop recurrent episodes; 10–15% progress to chronic low back pain (>12 weeks).  
- Return to work and normal activity within 4 weeks for most; poor prognostic factors include high pain intensity, fear avoidance beliefs, low socioeconomic status.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about red flags** — every new back pain patient needs a brief red flag history.  
- **Encourage early mobilisation** — give written advice (e.g. “Back Book” or NHS ‘Back pain’ leaflet).  
- **Use simple analgesia first** — paracetamol + NSAID combination is often sufficient; cycle them.  
- **Refer to physiotherapy early** if not improving by 4 weeks — prevents chronicity.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Ordering MRI for acute mechanical back pain** without red flags — exposes patient to unnecessary wait, cost, and incidental findings.  
- **Prescribing long-term opioids** — leads to tolerance, dependence, and hyperalgesia; evidence shows no benefit for chronic back pain.  
- **Advising bed rest** — worsens recovery, increases disability; advise staying active within pain limits.  
- **Missing cauda equina** by not checking perianal sensation and anal tone.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New leg weakness, numbness, or difficulty walking  
  - Loss of bladder/bowel control or saddle anaesthesia  
  - Worsening pain not controlled by simple analgesia after 48h  
  - Systemic symptoms (fever, chills, unexplained weight loss)  
  - Severe morning stiffness >30 min (may indicate inflammatory cause)

- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review in 2–4 weeks if not improving; earlier if red flag develops.  
  - Physiotherapy referral if pain persists >4–6 weeks.  
  - Emergency department if any red flag symptom appears.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/reg) immediately if:**  
  - Suspected cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia + urinary retention)  
  - Unexplained fever + severe back pain (infection)  
  - Acute leg weakness or sensory level (cord compression)  
  - Known cancer background with new back pain (malignancy)

- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (or rising) in setting of sepsis from spinal infection  
  - Hypovolaemic shock (suspected AAA rupture)  
  - GCS <13 or seizure (e.g. from meningitis/epidural abscess)

- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** — escalate to senior clinician for review within 30 min.  
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L** — consider sepsis from spinal source if febrile.  
  - **Post-void residual >200 mL** in setting of back pain — urgent urology + neurosurgery referral.

rawMarkdown:
# Low Back Pain — Mechanical

**One-liner:** Common musculoskeletal pain localised to lumbar region; usually self-limiting (<6 weeks), but must exclude red flags for serious spinal pathology.

## Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Cauda equina syndrome (CES):** *Saddle anaesthesia + acute urinary retention (post-void residual >200 mL)* — neurosurgical emergency within 24h to preserve continence & function.
- **Spinal infection (discitis/epidural abscess):** *Fever + severe unremitting back pain + raised CRP/ESR* — can progress to sepsis, paralysis, death.
- **Malignant cord compression:** *Known cancer history + progressive leg weakness/sensory level* — need urgent MRI + steroids ± radiotherapy.
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA):** *Pulsatile abdominal mass (if palpable) + hypotension/shock + back/flank pain* — immediate surgical referral; do not wait for CT.

## Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Axial low back pain, worse with movement (bending, lifting, twisting)  
  - No leg pain or radicular symptoms (if purely mechanical)  
  - Pain eased by lying flat or changing position  
  - Morning stiffness <30 min (if present, consider inflammatory back pain)  
  - No systemic symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss)

- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Normal neurological exam (motor, sensory, reflexes, perianal sensation, anal tone)  
  - Straight leg raise (SLR) negative bilaterally (if positive, suggests radiculopathy)  
  - Lumbar spine movement restricted by pain  
  - Paraspinal muscle spasm ± tenderness over facet joints  
  - No sacroiliac joint tenderness (may indicate SIJ dysfunction)  
  - No focal bone tenderness (if present, consider fracture)

## Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely**  
  1. Mechanical low back pain (non-specific) — no leg symptoms, no red flags, normal exam  
  2. Radiculopathy (sciatica) — leg pain/paraesthesia, positive SLR, may have reflex/sensory loss  
  3. Lumbar spinal stenosis — neurogenic claudication (relieved by sitting/leaning forward)  
  4. Inflammatory back pain (ankylosing spondylitis) — young adult, morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise, sacroiliitis on imaging  
  5. Sacroiliac joint dysfunction — unilateral buttock/leg pain, pain on SIJ provocation tests  
  6. **Red flag differentials** (cauda equina, infection, malignancy, fracture, AAA) — see Killers First

- **Splitter:**  
  Absence of leg pain + negative SLR → mechanical back pain (vs presence of leg pain + positive SLR → radiculopathy).

## Investigations

- **Bedside:**  
  - Full neurological exam including perianal sensation, anal tone, saddle area (every patient)  
  - Urine dipstick (UTI can mimic back pain in elderly)  
  - Pregnancy test (women of childbearing age) — refer to exclude ectopic if atypical pain  
  - Vital signs (temperature, HR, BP) — if febrile, think infection

- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, ESR — **only if red flags** (suspected infection, malignancy, inflammatory disease)  
  - U&E, bone profile — if suspicion of osteoporosis/fracture risk  
  - No routine bloods for mechanical back pain

- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** None — NICE recommends no imaging for acute non-specific low back pain without red flags.  
  - **Second-line (if red flags or >6 weeks with limited response to PT):**  
    - X-ray lumbar spine (AP + lateral) only if trauma, osteoporosis, or suspected fracture  
    - MRI spine (whole spine) for cauda equina, infection, malignancy, progressive neuro deficit  
    - CT spine if MRI contraindicated (e.g. pacemaker, claustrophobia) but less sensitive for soft tissue  
  - **Special test:** CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast if suspected AAA (but bedside US or CT angiogram if unstable)

- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
  - **Red flag checklist** (NICE): age <20 or >55, history of cancer, steroid use, IV drug use, fever, unexplained weight loss, neurological deficit, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention, band-like pain.  
  - No specific scoring system for mechanical back pain.

- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Thorough neurological examination including perianal sensory testing and anal tone** — omission of this can miss cauda equina syndrome until irreversible.

## Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Reassure: most acute back pain resolves in 4–6 weeks with simple measures.  
  - Advise **staying active** — avoid bed rest >24h.  
  - Simple analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO QDS ± NSAID (ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS or naproxen 250–500mg PO BD) — with food, check renal function & contraindications.  
  - Heat packs for local muscle spasm.  
  - No routine imaging; no opioids unless severe pain and short course (<1 week) [check local policy].

- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Rarely admitted for mechanical back pain alone — only if severe pain uncontrolled, red flags pending investigations, or severe functional limitation.  
  - Ensure mobility with physiotherapy review.  
  - Continue simple analgesia; avoid long-term opioids.  
  - If admitted for red flag workup, manage underlying cause (e.g. IV antibiotics for spinal infection).

- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Physiotherapy (exercise, manual therapy, directional preference exercises) — refer if pain persists >4–6 weeks or recurrent.  
  - Consider group exercise programme or structured back school.  
  - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for chronic pain.  
  - For chronic mechanical back pain (>12 weeks) with limited response: consider referral to pain clinic or multidisciplinary assessment.

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: nil specific unless on opioids (ensure hydration to prevent constipation).  
  - VTE prophylaxis: only if immobile or additional risk factors (e.g. age >60, obesity, surgery); low-molecular-weight heparin per local policy.  
  - Diet: full ward diet; encourage fibre if on opioids.  
  - Observations: routine 4-hourly unless red flag suspicion; if fever, consider 1-hourly.

## Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents:**  
  - Paracetamol: 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day) — first choice for mild–moderate pain.  
  - Ibuprofen: 400mg PO TDS (max 1.2g/day) — with or after food; avoid in asthma, GFR <30, heart failure, GI bleed history.  
  - Naproxen: 250–500mg PO BD (max 1g/day) — alternative to ibuprofen; same precautions.  
  - Codeine phosphate: 30–60mg PO Q6–8h PRN (short-term only if NSAID contraindicated or insufficient; add laxative). [Check local policy for dosing strong opioids.]

- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Tramadol 50–100mg PO Q4–6h (max 400mg/day) — avoid with SSRIs (serotonin syndrome risk).  
  - Amitriptyline 10–25mg PO nocte (for chronic pain, not acute) — start low, titrate; avoid in elderly, cardiac disease.  
  - Muscle relaxants (e.g. diazepam, baclofen) — not routinely recommended; risk of sedation, falls, dependence.

- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid in GFR <30, severe asthma, GI bleeding, uncontrolled hypertension, heart failure.  
  - Codeine/tramadol: avoid in opioid-tolerant patients, respiratory disorders, breastfeeding (codeine).  
  - Amitriptyline: caution with MAOIs, prolong QTc, history of seizures.

- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - NSAIDs: avoid if eGFR <30; dose reduction if 30–60; check baseline U&E.  
  - Paracetamol: safe in renal/hepatic impairment (avoid in severe liver disease).  
  - Codeine: reduce dose in renal impairment; avoid if hepatic impairment.  
  - Tramadol: reduce dose in renal/hepatic impairment [check local policy].

- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **NSAID overdose** or prescribing without checking renal function in elderly/dehydrated → acute kidney injury.  
  - **Paracetamol overdose** (unintentional or therapeutic) → hepatotoxicity; always check max daily dose (4g).  
  - **Codeine in ultra-rapid metabolisers** → opioid toxicity; avoid in children/breastfeeding.

## Complications

- **Acute:**  
  - Rare in mechanical back pain; progression to radiculopathy or neurogenic claudication.  
  - Medication-related: NSAID GI bleeding, analgesic-induced constipation.

- **Chronic:**  
  - Chronic low back pain (20–30% of acute cases) with functional limitation, disability, psychological distress.  
  - Work absence ≥4 weeks → increased risk of long-term disability.  
  - Opioid dependence if prescribed inappropriately.  
  - Deconditioning with prolonged inactivity.

## Prognosis

- 50% improve within 1 week; 90% by 6 weeks.  
- 20–30% develop recurrent episodes; 10–15% progress to chronic low back pain (>12 weeks).  
- Return to work and normal activity within 4 weeks for most; poor prognostic factors include high pain intensity, fear avoidance beliefs, low socioeconomic status.

## Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always ask about red flags** — every new back pain patient needs a brief red flag history.  
- **Encourage early mobilisation** — give written advice (e.g. “Back Book” or NHS ‘Back pain’ leaflet).  
- **Use simple analgesia first** — paracetamol + NSAID combination is often sufficient; cycle them.  
- **Refer to physiotherapy early** if not improving by 4 weeks — prevents chronicity.

## Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Ordering MRI for acute mechanical back pain** without red flags — exposes patient to unnecessary wait, cost, and incidental findings.  
- **Prescribing long-term opioids** — leads to tolerance, dependence, and hyperalgesia; evidence shows no benefit for chronic back pain.  
- **Advising bed rest** — worsens recovery, increases disability; advise staying active within pain limits.  
- **Missing cauda equina** by not checking perianal sensation and anal tone.

## Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**  
  - New leg weakness, numbness, or difficulty walking  
  - Loss of bladder/bowel control or saddle anaesthesia  
  - Worsening pain not controlled by simple analgesia after 48h  
  - Systemic symptoms (fever, chills, unexplained weight loss)  
  - Severe morning stiffness >30 min (may indicate inflammatory cause)

- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review in 2–4 weeks if not improving; earlier if red flag develops.  
  - Physiotherapy referral if pain persists >4–6 weeks.  
  - Emergency department if any red flag symptom appears.

## Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (SpR/reg) immediately if:**  
  - Suspected cauda equina syndrome (saddle anaesthesia + urinary retention)  
  - Unexplained fever + severe back pain (infection)  
  - Acute leg weakness or sensory level (cord compression)  
  - Known cancer background with new back pain (malignancy)

- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (or rising) in setting of sepsis from spinal infection  
  - Hypovolaemic shock (suspected AAA rupture)  
  - GCS <13 or seizure (e.g. from meningitis/epidural abscess)

- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** — escalate to senior clinician for review within 30 min.  
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L** — consider sepsis from spinal source if febrile.  
  - **Post-void residual >200 mL** in setting of back pain — urgent urology + neurosurgery referral.
